title: Traversing and COGO
description: Master the computational core of plane surveying: latitudes and departures, linear misclosure and relative precision, compass (Bowditch) and transit-rule traverse adjustment, coordinate geometry, the inverse problem, azimuth/bearing conversions, intersections, and area by coordinates (shoelace) — all with NCEES-approved calculator keystrokes.
key_topics: Latitude (ΔN) and departure (ΔE) of a course
Compass (Bowditch) rule and transit rule adjustment
DMS ↔ decimal degrees on the approved calculator

key_formulas.name: Forward coordinates
key_formulas.formula: N₂ = N₁ + L·cos(Az);  E₂ = E₁ + L·sin(Az)

key_formulas.name: Bearing→azimuth
key_formulas.formula: NE: Az=β; SE: Az=180−β; SW: Az=180+β; NW: Az=360−β

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Why this module is the heart of the FS exam

**Survey Computations and Computer Applications** is one of the two largest computational areas on the NCEES FS exam (17–26 of 110 questions), and **traversing and coordinate geometry (COGO) sit at its center**. Nearly every applied surveying problem — boundary, control, construction, topographic, route — eventually reduces to the same handful of operations you will drill here: turn a **bearing and distance** into a **latitude and departure**, accumulate them into **coordinates**, check the **closure**, **adjust** the traverse, and compute **area**.

A **traverse** is a series of connected lines (courses) whose lengths and directions are measured, used to establish horizontal control and to locate boundaries. The three families are:

- **Open traverse** — starts at a known point but ends at an unknown point; no closure check possible (avoid for control).
- **Closed-loop (polygon) traverse** — returns to its starting point; the latitudes must sum to zero and the departures must sum to zero, giving a closure check.
- **Closed-connecting (link) traverse** — starts and ends on two *different* known control points; closure is checked against the known coordinate difference.

The **two reciprocal operations** you must perform reflexively on the calculator are the **forward computation** (azimuth + distance → ΔN, ΔE → new coordinates, done with **polar→rectangular, P▸R**) and the **inverse** (two coordinates → distance + azimuth, done with **rectangular→polar, R▸P**). Master these two, plus the **shoelace** area formula, and you can solve the majority of the computational items on the exam.

This module assumes Module 3 (angles, bearings, azimuths, and DMS arithmetic). Here we put those directions to work building and adjusting traverses and computing coordinates and areas. **Every formula matches the NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.5** so the on-screen exam reference will look familiar.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## Latitude and departure

Every course has a **length L** and a **direction** (azimuth Az measured clockwise from north, or a quadrant bearing). Resolve the course onto the coordinate axes:

- **Latitude** = the north–south component = the change in **Northing**: `Lat = ΔN = L·cos(Az)`
- **Departure** = the east–west component = the change in **Easting**: `Dep = ΔE = L·sin(Az)`

**Sign convention is automatic when you use azimuth** (0°–360°): cos and sin already deliver the correct signs. North latitudes are +, south −; east departures +, west −. If you work with quadrant bearings, apply the sign by quadrant (N+/S−, E+/W−).

## Building coordinates (the forward computation)

Starting from a known point (N₁, E₁), the next point is:

`N₂ = N₁ + L·cos(Az)`   `E₂ = E₁ + L·sin(Az)`

Walk the traverse course by course, accumulating coordinates. On the calculator this is **polar→rectangular (P▸R)**: enter (L, Az) and read back (ΔN, ΔE) — note the surveying convention puts the **angle from north**, so the *x*-result is the departure and the *y*-result is the latitude.

## Closure of a traverse

For a **closed-loop** traverse the course components must close on themselves: ideally **ΣLat = 0** and **ΣDep = 0**. Measurement error makes them small nonzero values — the **closure in latitude** (`C_N = ΣLat`) and **closure in departure** (`C_E = ΣDep`). Combine them into the **linear misclosure**:

`e = √((ΣLat)² + (ΣDep)²)`

The quality of the work is the **relative precision** (precision ratio):

`RP = perimeter / e`, reported as **1:RP** (e.g., 1:12,000). Higher is better; boundary and control work commonly require 1:5,000 to 1:10,000 or better.

## Traverse adjustment

If the misclosure is acceptable, distribute it to force the traverse to close:

- **Compass (Bowditch) rule** — the most common. The correction to each course's latitude/departure is proportional to that course's **length** relative to the perimeter:
  - `C_lat,i = −(ΣLat)·(Lᵢ / P)`   `C_dep,i = −(ΣDep)·(Lᵢ / P)`
  - Assumes angles and distances are of comparable precision (typical of total-station work).
- **Transit rule** — the correction is proportional to the **magnitude of the latitude (or departure)** of the course rather than its length:
  - `C_lat,i = −(ΣLat)·(|Latᵢ| / Σ|Lat|)`   `C_dep,i = −(ΣDep)·(|Depᵢ| / Σ|Dep|)`
  - Used (historically) when angles are more precise than distances.
- **Least squares** — the rigorous method (Module on adjustments); compass/transit are the hand methods the FS tests.

After adjustment, the corrected latitudes sum to zero and corrected departures sum to zero, so the adjusted coordinates close.

## The inverse problem

Given two coordinates, recover the **distance and direction** between them:

- `ΔN = N₂ − N₁`, `ΔE = E₂ − E₁`
- **Distance** `D = √(ΔN² + ΔE²)`
- **Azimuth** `Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN)` normalized to 0°–360° (or `Az = arctan(ΔE/ΔN) + C`, with the handbook quadrant constant C: 0° in NE, 180° in SE and SW, 360° in NW).

On the calculator this is **rectangular→polar (R▸P)**: enter (ΔE, ΔN), read distance and angle.

## Intersections

- **Bearing–bearing**: two lines of known direction from two known points; solve the two `(E − E₀) = (N − N₀)·tan(Az)` equations for the common point.
- **Bearing–distance**: a direction from one point and a radius (distance) from another (one or two solutions).
- **Distance–distance**: two radii (circle–circle); two possible intersection points.

## Area by coordinates (shoelace)

Once every corner has coordinates, the enclosed area is, for points numbered in order around the polygon:

`A = ½·|Σ Xᵢ(Y_{i+1} − Y_{i−1})|`

equivalently `A = ½·|Σ (Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)|`. Divide ft² by **43,560** to get acres. This is the modern replacement for the legacy **DMD (double meridian distance)** method, which is still occasionally tested but gives the same result.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Components of a course

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Latitude (ΔN) | `Lat = L·cos(Az)` | + north, − south (automatic with azimuth) |
| Departure (ΔE) | `Dep = L·sin(Az)` | + east, − west (automatic with azimuth) |
| Course length (from components) | `L = √(Lat² + Dep²)` | the inverse distance |
| Course azimuth (from components) | `Az = atan2(Dep, Lat)`, normalize 0–360° | quadrant from signs of Lat, Dep |

## Forward (coordinates of a new point)

`N₂ = N₁ + L·cos(Az)`     `E₂ = E₁ + L·sin(Az)`

## Closure and precision (closed-loop)

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Closure in latitude | `C_N = ΣLat` |
| Closure in departure | `C_E = ΣDep` |
| Linear misclosure | `e = √((ΣLat)² + (ΣDep)²)` |
| Relative precision | `RP = P / e` → report **1:RP** |
| Azimuth of misclosure | `Az_e = atan2(−ΣDep, −ΣLat)` |

(P = perimeter = ΣL.)

## Compass (Bowditch) rule

`C_lat,i = −C_N·(Lᵢ / P)`     `C_dep,i = −C_E·(Lᵢ / P)`

Adjusted latitude = `Latᵢ + C_lat,i`; adjusted departure = `Depᵢ + C_dep,i`.

## Transit rule

`C_lat,i = −C_N·(|Latᵢ| / Σ|Lat|)`     `C_dep,i = −C_E·(|Depᵢ| / Σ|Dep|)`

## Inverse (rectangular → polar)

`ΔN = N₂ − N₁`,  `ΔE = E₂ − E₁`

`D = √(ΔN² + ΔE²) = hypot(ΔN, ΔE)`

`Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN)`, then add 360° if negative → 0°–360°.

Handbook quadrant form: `θ = arctan(ΔE/ΔN) + C`, with **C = 0° (NE), 180° (SE), 180° (SW), 360° (NW)**.

## Bearing ↔ azimuth

| Quadrant | Bearing → Azimuth | Azimuth → Bearing |
|---|---|---|
| NE | Az = β | β = Az |
| SE | Az = 180° − β | β = 180° − Az |
| SW | Az = 180° + β | β = Az − 180° |
| NW | Az = 360° − β | β = 360° − Az |

**Back-azimuth** = Az ± 180°.

## Area by coordinates (shoelace)

`A = ½·|Σ (Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)|`   (close the polygon: last point connects to first)

Acres = A(ft²) / 43,560.

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## A labeled 4-leg closed traverse

The traverse **A → B → C → D → A** below is computed throughout these examples. Bearings/azimuths and distances are labeled on each leg.

<svg width="460" height="360" viewBox="0 0 460 360" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #ccc">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#1a5"/></marker>
  </defs>
  <!-- north arrow -->
  <line x1="40" y1="60" x2="40" y2="20" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>
  <text x="33" y="16" font-size="13" font-family="sans-serif">N</text>
  <!-- traverse polygon: A bottom-left, B bottom-right, C upper-right, D upper-left -->
  <polygon points="90,300 360,300 410,110 140,150" fill="#eef6ff" stroke="#1c4e80" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- vertices -->
  <circle cx="90" cy="300" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <circle cx="360" cy="300" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <circle cx="410" cy="110" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <circle cx="140" cy="150" r="4" fill="#c0392b"/>
  <text x="72" y="318" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">A</text>
  <text x="364" y="318" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">B</text>
  <text x="416" y="108" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">C</text>
  <text x="118" y="146" font-size="14" font-weight="bold">D</text>
  <!-- leg labels -->
  <text x="190" y="320" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">A→B: N 90°00′ E (Az 90°), 600.00 ft</text>
  <text x="372" y="210" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">B→C: N 14°02′ W</text>
  <text x="372" y="226" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">(Az 345°57′47″), 412.31 ft</text>
  <text x="150" y="230" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">C→D: S 81°28′ W</text>
  <text x="150" y="246" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">(Az 261°28′, 273.0 ft)</text>
  <text x="50" y="230" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">D→A:</text>
  <text x="30" y="246" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">S 18°26′ E,</text>
  <text x="30" y="262" font-size="12" fill="#1c4e80">158.1 ft</text>
</svg>

For clean arithmetic we use these legs (azimuths from north):

| Leg | Azimuth | Distance L (ft) |
|---|---|---|
| A→B | 90°00′00″ | 600.00 |
| B→C | 345°57′45″ | 412.31 |
| C→D | 261°52′12″ | 282.84 |
| D→A | 198°26′06″ | 316.23 |

---

## Example 1 — Latitude & departure of each leg

**`Lat = L·cos(Az)`, `Dep = L·sin(Az)`.**

- **A→B** (Az 90°): Lat = 600·cos 90° = **0.00**; Dep = 600·sin 90° = **+600.00**
- **B→C** (Az 345.9625°): Lat = 412.31·cos 345.9625° = **+400.00**; Dep = 412.31·sin 345.9625° = **−100.00**
- **C→D** (Az 261.8700°): Lat = 282.84·cos 261.87° = **−40.00**; Dep = 282.84·sin 261.87° = **−280.00**
- **D→A** (Az 198.4350°): Lat = 316.23·cos 198.435° = **−300.00**; Dep = 316.23·sin 198.435° = **−100.00**

**Check:** ΣLat = 0 + 400 − 40 − 300 = **+60.00 ft**; ΣDep = 600 − 100 − 280 − 100 = **+120.00 ft** (these are the misclosures used in Example 4).

**Calculator (TI-36X Pro):** put the calc in DEGREE mode. For a leg, key `L  ×  cos(  Az  )  =` for the latitude and `L  ×  sin(  Az  )  =` for the departure. Convert a DMS azimuth first with the `°’”` (DMS) entry, e.g. `345°57′45″` entered directly, then `cos`. **Casio fx-991:** identical; enter the DMS angle with the `°’”` key. **HP-35S:** `345.5745  →HR` converts H.MMSS to decimal degrees, then `COS` / `SIN`.

---

## Example 2 — The inverse: distance & azimuth between two points (R▸P)

Point P1 = (N 5,000.00, E 5,000.00); Point P2 = (N 5,400.00, E 4,900.00). Find distance and azimuth P1→P2.

1. `ΔN = 5400 − 5000 = +400.00`;  `ΔE = 4900 − 5000 = −100.00`
2. **Distance** `D = √(400² + 100²) = √170000 = 412.31 ft`
3. **Azimuth** `Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) = atan2(−100, +400) = −14.036° → +345.96°` (i.e. **345°57′45″**). ΔN +, ΔE − ⇒ NW quadrant, so bearing **N 14°02′15″ W**.

**Calculator R▸P (TI-36X Pro):** `2nd [R▸P]` opens `R▸Pr(` and `R▸Pθ(`. Key `R▸Pr( ΔE , ΔN )` → **412.31** (distance); `R▸Pθ( ΔE , ΔN )` → **−14.04°**, add 360° → 345.96°, then `▸DMS`. (Enter ΔE first as *x*, ΔN as *y* per the surveying convention.) **Casio fx-991:** `Pol(ΔE, ΔN)` returns r→variable X and θ→variable Y in one step. **HP-35S:** enter ΔN, ΔE and press `→POL`.

---

## Example 3 — Coordinates of a point from azimuth + distance (P▸R)

From A = (N 5,000.00, E 5,000.00), run **Az 198°26′06″ for 316.23 ft** to point D. Find D's coordinates.

1. `ΔN (Lat) = 316.23·cos 198.435° = −300.00`;  `ΔE (Dep) = 316.23·sin 198.435° = −100.00`
2. `N_D = 5000 + (−300.00) = 4,700.00`;  `E_D = 5000 + (−100.00) = 4,900.00`

**Calculator P▸R (TI-36X Pro):** `2nd [P▸R]` gives `P▸Rx(` and `P▸Ry(`. Key `P▸Rx( L , Az )` → **departure −100.00** (the *x* result); `P▸Ry( L , Az )` → **latitude −300.00** (the *y* result). Add to the start coordinates. **Casio fx-991:** `Rec(L, Az)` returns x (=ΔE) and y (=ΔN). **HP-35S:** enter L, Az; `→REC`.

---

## Example 4 — Misclosure & relative precision

From Example 1, ΣLat = +60.00 ft, ΣDep = +120.00 ft, perimeter P = 600.00 + 412.31 + 282.84 + 316.23 = 1,611.38 ft.

1. **Linear misclosure** `e = √(60² + 120²) = √(3600 + 14400) = √18000 = 134.16 ft` (this exaggerated example shows the method; real misclosures are tenths of a foot).
2. **Relative precision** `RP = P / e = 1611.38 / 134.16 = 12.01` → **≈ 1:12**. (A real 0.13-ft misclosure on this perimeter would give 1611/0.13 ≈ **1:12,400**.)

**Calculator:** misclosure is just `hypot(ΣLat, ΣDep)` — on the TI use `R▸Pr(ΣDep, ΣLat)` to get e in one keystroke. Report relative precision by dividing and rounding to a clean 1:N.

---

## Example 5 — Area by coordinates (shoelace)

Four corners (closed-loop): A(N 5000, E 5000), B(N 5000, E 5600), C(N 5400, E 5500), D(N 4700, E 4900). Using X = Easting, Y = Northing, and the cross form `A = ½|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)|`:

| i | Xᵢ (E) | Yᵢ (N) | X_{i+1} | Y_{i+1} | Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} | X_{i+1}·Yᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5000 | 5000 | 5600 | 5000 | 25,000,000 | 28,000,000 |
| B | 5600 | 5000 | 5500 | 5400 | 30,240,000 | 27,500,000 |
| C | 5500 | 5400 | 4900 | 4700 | 25,850,000 | 29,160,000 |
| D | 4900 | 4700 | 5000 | 5000 | 24,500,000 | 23,500,000 |

Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1}) = 105,590,000; Σ(X_{i+1}·Yᵢ) = 108,160,000. Difference = −2,570,000.

`A = ½·|−2,570,000| = 1,285,000 ft²`  → **÷ 43,560 = 29.50 acres.**

**Calculator tip:** there is no one-button shoelace on the TI/Casio — set it up as a running sum in the home screen, or use the matrix/list features to enter the products. The HP-35S survey program book includes a stored **AREA** routine that accepts sequential N,E inputs and returns acreage directly.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## Strategy for the computations area

- **COGO is the most learnable, highest-density point source on the FS.** The same two operations (forward P▸R, inverse R▸P) plus shoelace area cover most items. Drill them until they are reflexive — you should not be *thinking* about which key, only about the geometry.
- **Pace:** ~2.9 min/question overall. A clean inverse or a single latitude/departure should take under a minute; flag a long multi-leg traverse-adjustment problem and bank quick ones first.
- **Sketch first.** A 30-second vicinity sketch with the rough azimuth of each leg catches sign and quadrant errors before they cost you.

## Approved-calculator tricks (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991 / HP-35S)

- **Always confirm DEGREE mode** before any trig. A traverse in radian mode is the classic silent blunder.
- **R▸P does the inverse in two keystrokes:** distance = `R▸Pr(ΔE, ΔN)`, azimuth = `R▸Pθ(ΔE, ΔN)`. On Casio, `Pol(ΔE, ΔN)` returns both r and θ at once (stored in X and Y).
- **P▸R does the forward computation:** departure = `P▸Rx(L, Az)`, latitude = `P▸Ry(L, Az)`. On Casio, `Rec(L, Az)`.
- **Surveying convention:** enter ΔE (departure) as *x* and ΔN (latitude) as *y*, because azimuth is measured from **north (the y-axis)**. Get this backwards and your azimuth is reflected.
- **Normalize azimuth:** if R▸Pθ / atan2 returns a negative angle, **add 360°**; if it exceeds 360°, subtract 360°.
- **DMS ↔ decimal:** TI uses `°’”` for entry and `▸DMS` for output; Casio the same; HP-35S uses `→HR` (to decimal) and `→HMS` (to D.MMSS). Convert *before* trig and back *after* an inverse.
- **Store and recall** running northing/easting totals (TI `STO`/`RCL`, HP register chaining) so you don't re-key coordinates leg to leg — this both saves time and prevents transcription blunders.
- **Misclosure in one shot:** `e = R▸Pr(ΣDep, ΣLat)`.

## Common traps

- **Latitude uses cosine, departure uses sine** — memorize "**L**atitude–co**s**, **D**eparture–**s**in." Swapping them is the #1 COGO error.
- **Bowditch vs. transit:** Bowditch weights by **course length**; transit weights by **latitude/departure magnitude**. The exam may name the rule — use the one asked.
- **Sign of the correction:** the correction is the **negative** of the closure distributed out (you are removing the misclosure).
- **Relative precision is reported as 1:N**, with the **larger** number being **better**. Don't invert it.
- **Area sign:** the shoelace sum is negative for clockwise vertex order — always take the **absolute value**, then divide by 43,560 for acres.
- **Units:** watch ft vs. m and US survey-foot vs. international foot when a problem mixes systems.

## How the handbook helps

The NCEES FS Reference Handbook v2.5 gives the **inverse** (distance + the quadrant-constant azimuth form), **area by coordinates**, **straight-line / coordinate-geometry** relations, and the **bearing/azimuth** conventions verbatim. Know *where* each lives (Mathematical & Surveying-Related Formulas → Area formulas, and the Coordinate Geometry section) so you can confirm a formula in seconds instead of deriving it under time pressure.

questions.question_text: A closed-loop traverse returns to its starting point. In the absence of error, what must be true of the latitudes and departures of its courses?
options: ΣLat and ΣDep each equal the misclosure
questions.explanation: A loop traverse starts and ends at the same point, so the north–south components (latitudes) must net to zero and the east–west components (departures) must net to zero. Nonzero sums are the closure in latitude and departure, whose vector sum is the linear misclosure.

questions.question_text: The latitude of a course is computed as:
questions.explanation: Latitude is the north–south (Northing) component of a course: Lat = L·cos(Az). The departure is the east–west component, L·sin(Az). Remember 'Latitude–cosine, Departure–sine.'

questions.question_text: A course has length 250.00 ft and azimuth 120°00′00″. What are its latitude and departure?
questions.explanation: Lat = 250·cos120° = 250·(−0.5) = −125.00 ft (south). Dep = 250·sin120° = 250·(0.8660) = +216.51 ft (east). Azimuth 120° is in the SE quadrant, so latitude is negative and departure positive.

questions.question_text: Which traverse type starts and ends on two DIFFERENT known control points and is checked against the known coordinate difference?
questions.explanation: A closed-connecting or 'link' traverse begins on one known point and closes on a second known point; the computed coordinate difference is compared with the known difference to assess closure. A loop returns to its start; an open traverse has no closure check.

questions.question_text: Linear misclosure of a closed traverse is computed from the closure in latitude (ΣLat) and departure (ΣDep) as:
questions.explanation: The misclosure is the magnitude of the closure vector: e = √((ΣLat)² + (ΣDep)²) = hypot(ΣLat, ΣDep). On a calculator it is the R▸P distance of (ΣDep, ΣLat).

questions.question_text: A traverse has a perimeter of 2,400.00 ft and a linear misclosure of 0.20 ft. The relative precision is most nearly:
questions.explanation: RP = perimeter / misclosure = 2400 / 0.20 = 12,000, reported as 1:12,000. The larger the denominator, the better the precision.

questions.question_text: Under the compass (Bowditch) rule, the correction applied to a course's latitude is proportional to:
options: the length of that course relative to the perimeter
the magnitude of that course's latitude relative to Σ|Lat|
the number of courses in the traverse
the area enclosed by the traverse
questions.correct_answer: the length of that course relative to the perimeter
questions.explanation: The Bowditch (compass) rule distributes the misclosure in proportion to course length: C_lat,i = −(ΣLat)·(Lᵢ/P). The transit rule instead uses the magnitude of the latitude/departure of each course.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes the compass (Bowditch) rule from the transit rule?
options: Bowditch weights corrections by course length; the transit rule weights by latitude/departure magnitude
Bowditch weights by latitude/departure magnitude; the transit rule weights by course length
Both distribute the misclosure equally to every course
Bowditch applies only to angles and the transit rule only to distances
questions.correct_answer: Bowditch weights corrections by course length; the transit rule weights by latitude/departure magnitude
questions.explanation: The Bowditch rule assumes angles and distances are equally precise and distributes the closure by course length. The transit rule assumes angles are more precise than distances and distributes by the magnitude of each course's latitude (or departure).

questions.question_text: Point A is (N 1,000.00, E 1,000.00) and point B is (N 1,300.00, E 1,400.00). The horizontal distance A→B is:
For the inverse from A(N 1,000, E 1,000) to B(N 1,300, E 1,400), the azimuth A→B is most nearly:
questions.explanation: Az = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) = atan2(400, 300) = 53.13° ≈ 53°08′. Both ΔN and ΔE are positive (NE quadrant), so the azimuth equals the bearing angle N 53°08′ E.

questions.question_text: Why are ΔE entered as x and ΔN entered as y when using the calculator's rectangular→polar function for a survey inverse?
options: Azimuth is measured clockwise from north (the y-axis), so the angle returned is the azimuth directly
It does not matter; the result is the same either way
Because departure is always larger than latitude
To force the calculator into radian mode
questions.correct_answer: Azimuth is measured clockwise from north (the y-axis), so the angle returned is the azimuth directly
questions.explanation: Survey azimuth is referenced to north. By placing ΔN on the y-axis and ΔE on the x-axis, the polar angle the calculator returns (measured from +y for atan2(x,y)) is the azimuth. Swapping them reflects the angle and gives the wrong direction.

questions.question_text: A bearing of S 40°00′ W expressed as an azimuth from north is:
questions.explanation: In the SW quadrant, Az = 180° + bearing angle = 180° + 40° = 220°.

questions.question_text: The back-azimuth of a line whose forward azimuth is 245°30′ is:
questions.explanation: Back-azimuth = forward azimuth ± 180°. 245°30′ − 180° = 65°30′ (subtract because the result must stay in 0°–360°).

questions.question_text: Forward computation: from P(N 2,000.00, E 3,000.00), a course of azimuth 60°00′00″ and length 400.00 ft reaches point Q. The northing of Q is most nearly:
The area by coordinates (shoelace) of a closed polygon is computed as:
questions.explanation: The shoelace (coordinate) formula sums the cross-products of successive vertices, halves the magnitude, and the polygon must be closed (last vertex connects to the first). It equals the handbook form A = ½|ΣXᵢ(Y_{i+1} − Y_{i−1})|.

questions.question_text: A parcel computes to 217,800 ft² by coordinates. Its area in acres is:
After applying the compass rule, the sum of the corrected (adjusted) latitudes of a closed-loop traverse should equal:
questions.explanation: Adjustment forces closure: the corrections are designed so that ΣLat and ΣDep of the adjusted courses each become zero, allowing the traverse to close on itself.

questions.question_text: Which calculator operation directly yields the distance and azimuth between two known coordinates?
questions.explanation: R▸P converts the rectangular components (ΔE, ΔN) into polar form (distance, angle) — exactly the inverse problem. P▸R does the opposite (forward) computation: azimuth + distance into ΔE, ΔN.

questions.question_text: A course azimuth computed by atan2(ΔE, ΔN) comes out as −47.0°. The correct azimuth (0°–360°) is:
questions.explanation: A negative azimuth is normalized by adding 360°: −47.0° + 360° = 313.0° (a NW-quadrant direction, consistent with ΔN +, ΔE −).

questions.question_text: Which intersection problem can have TWO valid solution points?
options: Bearing–bearing intersection of two non-parallel lines
An inverse between two fixed points
Forward computation from azimuth and distance
questions.explanation: Two circles (distance–distance) generally intersect at two points, so the surveyor must pick the geometrically correct one. Two non-parallel lines (bearing–bearing) intersect at exactly one point; the inverse and forward computations are unique.

questions.question_text: An open traverse is generally avoided for control work because:
options: it provides no check on the measurements (no closure)
it cannot be computed with a calculator
its latitudes always sum to a large value
questions.correct_answer: it provides no check on the measurements (no closure)
questions.explanation: An open traverse ends at an unknown point, so there is no second known value to check against — undetected blunders propagate. Loop and link traverses both provide a closure check.

questions.question_text: True or False: When computing area by coordinates, the shoelace sum is taken as an absolute value because a clockwise ordering of vertices produces a negative sum.
questions.explanation: True. The signed shoelace sum is positive for counterclockwise vertex order and negative for clockwise order; the absolute value gives the physical area regardless of direction.

questions.question_text: A misclosure has ΣLat = −0.12 ft and ΣDep = +0.05 ft. The linear misclosure is most nearly:
True or False: The relative precision 1:8,000 represents BETTER work than 1:5,000.
questions.explanation: True. Relative precision is misclosure per unit of perimeter expressed as 1:N; a larger N means the misclosure is a smaller fraction of the perimeter, i.e., better work.

questions.question_text: In the handbook quadrant form Az = arctan(ΔE/ΔN) + C, the constant C for a course in the SE quadrant (ΔN negative, ΔE positive) is:
questions.explanation: Per the NCEES handbook, C = 0° in NE (ΔN+, ΔE+), 180° in SE and SW (ΔN negative), and 360° in NW (ΔN+, ΔE−). For SE, C = 180°.

questions.question_text: A course of latitude +180.00 ft and departure −240.00 ft has what length?

problem_templates.name: Latitude of a course
problem_templates.question_template: A traverse course has a length of {{dist:f2}} ft and an azimuth of {{az:f2}}° (measured clockwise from north). Compute the LATITUDE (ΔN, north–south component) of the course in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The latitude is the north–south component of the course: Lat = L·cos(Az) = {{dist:f2}} × cos({{az:f2}}°) = {{_answer}} ft. Use the cosine for latitude (sine gives the departure). The sign is automatic from the azimuth.

problem_templates.name: Departure of a course
problem_templates.question_template: A traverse course has a length of {{dist:f2}} ft and an azimuth of {{az:f2}}° (clockwise from north). Compute the DEPARTURE (ΔE, east–west component) of the course in feet.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The departure is the east–west component of the course: Dep = L·sin(Az) = {{dist:f2}} × sin({{az:f2}}°) = {{_answer}} ft. Use the sine for departure (cosine gives the latitude). The sign follows from the azimuth automatically.

problem_templates.name: Inverse between two points (distance)
problem_templates.question_template: Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}). Point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Compute the horizontal distance A→B (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Use rectangular→polar. ΔN = {{dN:f2}}, ΔE = {{dE:f2}}; the distance is √(ΔN² + ΔE²) = {{_answer}} ft. On the TI, R▸Pr(ΔE, ΔN) returns this in one keystroke.

problem_templates.name: Inverse between two points (azimuth)
problem_templates.question_template: Point A is at (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}). Point B is at (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}). Compute the azimuth (clockwise from north, 0°–360°) of the line A→B, in decimal degrees.

solution_steps_template.title: Azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN), normalized
solution_steps_template.result_template: {{_answer}}° (add 360° if the raw angle is negative)

problem_templates.explanation_template: Azimuth = atan2(ΔE, ΔN) referenced to north. Raw value = {{azRaw:f2}}°; normalized to 0°–360° gives {{_answer}}°. The signs of ΔN ({{dN:f2}}) and ΔE ({{dE:f2}}) place the line in its quadrant. On the TI, R▸Pθ(ΔE, ΔN) then add 360° if negative.

problem_templates.name: Coordinates of a point from azimuth + distance
problem_templates.question_template: Starting at point P (N {{n1:f2}}, E {{e1:f2}}), a course of azimuth {{az:f2}}° (clockwise from north) and length {{dist:f2}} ft is run to point Q. Compute the NORTHING (N) of point Q (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Forward (polar→rectangular) computation: the latitude is L·cos(Az) = {{dist:f2}} × cos({{az:f2}}°) = {{lat:f2}} ft. Add to the starting northing: N_Q = {{n1:f2}} + {{lat:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. (The easting would use L·sin(Az).)

problem_templates.name: Coordinates of a point — easting from azimuth + distance
problem_templates.question_template: Starting at point P (N {{n1:f2}}, E {{e1:f2}}), a course of azimuth {{az:f2}}° (clockwise from north) and length {{dist:f2}} ft is run to point Q. Compute the EASTING (E) of point Q (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Forward (polar→rectangular) computation: the departure is L·sin(Az) = {{dist:f2}} × sin({{az:f2}}°) = {{dep:f2}} ft. Add to the starting easting: E_Q = {{e1:f2}} + {{dep:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft. (The northing would use L·cos(Az).)

problem_templates.name: Linear misclosure of a closed traverse
problem_templates.question_template: A closed-loop traverse has a closure in latitude ΣLat = {{sumLat:f3}} ft and a closure in departure ΣDep = {{sumDep:f3}} ft. Compute the linear misclosure (ft).
problem_templates.explanation_template: The linear misclosure is the magnitude of the closure vector: e = √((ΣLat)² + (ΣDep)²) = √(({{sumLat:f3}})² + ({{sumDep:f3}})²) = {{_answer}} ft. On the TI this is R▸Pr(ΣDep, ΣLat).

problem_templates.name: Relative precision of a traverse
problem_templates.question_template: A closed traverse has a perimeter of {{perim:f2}} ft and a linear misclosure of {{misc:f3}} ft. Express the relative precision as 1:N — report the value of N (rounded to the nearest whole number).
problem_templates.explanation_template: Relative precision = perimeter / misclosure = {{perim:f2}} / {{misc:f3}} = {{_answer}}, reported as 1:{{_answer}}. A larger N indicates better (tighter) work.

problem_templates.name: Area by coordinates (triangle, shoelace)
problem_templates.question_template: A triangular parcel has corners A (N {{nA:f2}}, E {{eA:f2}}), B (N {{nB:f2}}, E {{eB:f2}}), and C (N {{nC:f2}}, E {{eC:f2}}), taken in order. Compute the enclosed area by coordinates (shoelace), in square feet.

solution_steps_template.title: Shoelace cross-products
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Sum the three cross-products, halve the absolute value

problem_templates.explanation_template: Apply the shoelace formula with X = Easting, Y = Northing, vertices in order A→B→C: A = ½|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)| = {{_answer}} ft². Divide by 43,560 to convert to acres. The absolute value handles clockwise vs. counterclockwise ordering.

problem_templates.name: Bearing to azimuth conversion
problem_templates.question_template: A line has the quadrant bearing S {{beta:f2}}° W. Express this as an azimuth (clockwise from north, 0°–360°), in decimal degrees.
problem_templates.explanation_template: For a SW-quadrant bearing, the azimuth = 180° + the bearing angle = 180° + {{beta:f2}}° = {{_answer}}°. (NE: Az = β; SE: 180 − β; SW: 180 + β; NW: 360 − β.)

mock_questions.question_text: A traverse course is 350.00 ft long with an azimuth of 300°00′00″. Its departure (ΔE) is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: Dep = L·sin(Az) = 350·sin300° = 350·(−0.8660) = −303.11 ft (west, NW quadrant). The latitude would be 350·cos300° = +175.00 ft.

mock_questions.question_text: Point P (N 4,200.00, E 6,500.00) and point Q (N 3,900.00, E 6,900.00). The horizontal distance P→Q is most nearly:
For the line from P (N 4,200, E 6,500) to Q (N 3,900, E 6,900), the azimuth P→Q is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: ΔN = −300, ΔE = +400 (SE quadrant). Az = atan2(400, −300) = 126.87° ≈ 126°52′, equivalently S 53°08′ E.

mock_questions.question_text: A closed-loop traverse has ΣLat = +0.18 ft and ΣDep = −0.24 ft, with a perimeter of 1,800.00 ft. The relative precision is most nearly:
Using the compass (Bowditch) rule, the latitude correction for a 400.00-ft course in a traverse with perimeter 2,000.00 ft and ΣLat = +0.10 ft is:
mock_questions.explanation: C_lat = −(ΣLat)·(L/P) = −(0.10)·(400/2000) = −0.10·0.20 = −0.02 ft. The correction is negative to remove the positive closure.

mock_questions.question_text: A four-corner parcel has coordinates (E, N): (0, 0), (600, 0), (600, 400), (0, 400). Its area by coordinates is:
mock_questions.explanation: This is a 600 × 400 rectangle; the shoelace formula gives ½|Σ(Xᵢ·Y_{i+1} − X_{i+1}·Yᵢ)| = 240,000 ft² = 5.51 acres.

mock_questions.question_text: Which calculator function returns the coordinates of a new point directly from a course azimuth and distance?
mock_questions.explanation: P▸R converts (distance, azimuth) into rectangular components (ΔE, ΔN), which are added to the starting coordinates. R▸P performs the reverse (inverse) computation.

mock_questions.question_text: The bearing N 27°15′ W expressed as an azimuth is:
mock_questions.explanation: For a NW-quadrant bearing, Az = 360° − bearing = 360° − 27°15′ = 332°45′.

mock_questions.question_text: A course has latitude −150.00 ft and departure +150.00 ft. Its length and azimuth are most nearly:
Two adjoining sequential deeds are plotted by coordinates and the surveyor needs the strip area between them. The most direct computation, once all corner coordinates are known, is:
options: Area by coordinates (shoelace) on the strip polygon
mock_questions.correct_answer: Area by coordinates (shoelace) on the strip polygon
mock_questions.explanation: With all corners reduced to coordinates, the planar area of any closed figure is computed directly by the shoelace (coordinate) method. End-area and prismoidal formulas are volume methods; degree of curve is a horizontal-curve quantity.

mock_questions.question_text: An open traverse differs from a closed traverse principally in that an open traverse:
options: provides no closure check on its measurements
cannot be run with a total station
must be adjusted by least squares
mock_questions.correct_answer: provides no closure check on its measurements
mock_questions.explanation: An open traverse ends at an unknown point, so there is no independent value to check against and blunders go undetected. This is why it is avoided for control surveys.

mock_questions.question_text: A closed-loop traverse has a linear misclosure of 0.40 ft over a 5,000.00-ft perimeter. The achieved relative precision, and whether it meets a 1:10,000 requirement, is:
mock_questions.explanation: RP = perimeter / misclosure = 5,000 / 0.40 = 12,500, i.e. 1:12,500. Since 12,500 ≥ 10,000, the traverse meets the 1:10,000 standard (equivalently, the allowable misclosure is 5,000/10,000 = 0.50 ft and 0.40 ft is within it).
